title: Pinch-pot tea-light holder
subtitle: A small pinch pot with pierced holes that project candlelight
excerpt: A pinch-pot tea-light holder is a first pottery project that produces something useful on the same day. The pot is formed from a single ball of clay, pierced while leather-hard to let candlelight through, and sealed once bone-dry. No kiln or wheel is needed. Air-dry clay handles the heat of a tea light safely when the walls are at least 8 mm thick.
sourceNotes: Pinch-pot technique. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: A
pinch-pot

tea-light holder is built from a single ball of clay, requires no kiln or wheel, and can be finished in two sittings over a day and a half. The tea light sits inside the pot and its light passes through holes pierced in the walls. Keep the walls at least 8 mm thick throughout: walls thinner than 8 mm can crack from the heat gradient between the candle side and the cool outer surface.

attrs.body: Use tea lights only; walls must be at least 8 mm thick throughout.
attrs.title: Heat safety

content.text: What you need
Forming the pot
Step 1: Form the ball and start pinching

Roll 200 g of clay into a smooth ball, about 7 cm across. Press the thumb of your dominant hand straight down into the centre of the ball to within 10 mm of the base: this leaves a 10-mm base that will not crack from the tea-light heat. Begin pinching the walls between thumb inside and fingers outside, rotating the ball a quarter-turn after each pinch.
Step 2: Keep the opening narrow enough for the tea light

A standard tea-light cup is about 38 mm in diameter. Keep the opening of the pot at 40 to 42 mm so the tea light can be placed inside but sits below the rim. If the opening becomes too wide, gently press the rim inward between both hands. The walls throughout should be 8 to 10 mm thick.
Step 3: Smooth and allow to reach leather-hard
Smooth the outside of the pot with a damp sponge. Place it on its side on the canvas board and leave uncovered for two to three hours until it reaches
leather-hard
. The pot should hold its shape when set upright without collapsing.
Step 4: Pierce the pattern

Use the needle tool to push holes through the walls in your chosen pattern. Work at leather-hard stage, not wet: wet clay tears and deforms around the hole. Push the needle straight through in one firm movement rather than drilling in circles, which creates a ragged edge. A simple scattering of small round holes produces a starfield effect. Rows of diamond-shaped holes (made by pushing the needle at an angle) or geometric patterns also work well. Keep holes at least 5 mm from each other and from the rim.
Drying and sealing

Allow the holder to dry to bone-dry, set upright. Full drying takes one to two days. Once bone-dry, seal the outside with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1 with water, or with an acrylic varnish. Do not seal the inside; the tea light needs direct contact with the clay for stability.
Variations
Cut a star or crescent moon shape with the fettling knife instead of piercing holes: larger cutouts throw more light.
Press a stamp into the outside surface before piercing for layered decoration.
Common problems

attrs.intro: Issues when making the tea-light holder.

items.fix: Leave for another hour before piercing. At leather-hard, the needle pushes through cleanly.
items.symptom: Holes tear and deform when pierced

items.fix: Test wall thickness with the needle by pressing gently: it should meet resistance at 8-10 mm. Thin areas can be thickened by pressing a small pad of fresh clay onto the interior and blending.
items.cause: Uneven pinching, more on some sides than others.

items.fix: On future builds, check base thickness with the needle before piercing the walls. Seal the cracked base with a thin layer of PVA for cosmetic repair.
items.symptom: Base cracks after the first use with a candle
